In a podcast discussion with Yalda Hakim on 'The World with Richard Engel and Yalda Hakim,' Engel describes his recent visit to Gaza, which was facilitated exclusively by the Israeli Defense Forces (IDF), as international journalists are not permitted independent entry into the Strip. During the tour, he was shown a Hamas tunnel that the IDF claims was actively used until recently, indicating ongoing security concerns. Engel highlights the widespread and near-total destruction he witnessed across much of Gaza, painting a grim picture of devastated infrastructure and landscapes. The discussion addresses key lingering issues: whether Hamas continues to operate in the area, the reasons Israeli troops have not fully withdrawn from all parts of Gaza, and the status of Palestinians displaced from their homes

item.title: Trump Faces Limited Options as Pressure on Iran Fails
item.description: The United States, under President Donald Trump, has amassed significant military forces near Iran in an effort to compel the Iranian government to negotiate on terms favorable to Washington. Despite warnings and shows of naval strength, Tehran appears unshaken and continues to resist US pressure. Trump's chief negotiator, Steve Witkoff, acknowledged that even with overwhelming American firepower, Iran has not capitulated. Analysts suggest the president faces three main courses of action. The first is limited, targeted strikes, which may be insufficient to force compliance and could be perceived as a miscalculation. The second is an all-out military attack, but US forces may not have enough capacity to sustain a prolonged campaign, and Iran could retaliate with ballistic missiles. The third option is to step back and avoid military engagement, though this could be politically damaging and undermine US credibility internationally. With upcoming Geneva talks offering a potential diplomat

item.title: Police End Searches at Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor's Former Royal Lodge as Parliament Supports Release of Trade Envoy Appointment Files
item.description: Thames Valley Police have completed their searches at Royal Lodge in Windsor Great Park, Berkshire, the former residence of Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor, following his arrest last Thursday at Sandringham on his 66th birthday. The former prince, once known as Prince Andrew, was detained for 11 hours on suspicion of misconduct in public office, specifically allegations that he shared sensitive information with the late convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein during his tenure as UK trade envoy from 2001 to 2011. He was released under investigation and has consistently denied any wrongdoing related to Epstein. Assistant Chief Constable Oliver Wright confirmed the search conclusion, noting the investigation remains active and updates will be limited to allow progress without interference, acknowledging significant public interest. Separately, on Tuesday, UK MPs unanimously supported a motion to release government files concerning Andrew's appointment as trade envoy, without requiring a formal

item.title: Brent Council Enforcement Officers Tackle Persistent Fly-Tipping in London Borough
item.description: The London Borough of Brent has been highlighted as England's fly-tipping capital, recording around 35,000 incidents in a recent year, leading to widespread rubbish accumulation including fridges, sofas, TVs, mattresses, and bags of waste on streets near landmarks like Wembley Stadium. Long-time resident Robert Hall describes the area as an 'eyesore' and expresses embarrassment about inviting visitors, noting that friends avoid coming due to the filth. Brent Council’s enforcement team, led by supervisor Anca Pricop, actively patrols streets, sifting through dumped waste for clues like prescription labels, delivery tags, or vehicle details to trace culprits. Officers emphasize the importance of households verifying waste removal services' licenses, as unlicensed operators often dump illegally, sometimes on the same street. Fines are issued to responsible parties when identified, and the council has seized and crushed vehicles involved in dumping. Efforts including new community skips ha

item.title: Only Three NHS Trusts Met 85% Cancer Treatment Target in 2025, Data Shows Wide Regional Gaps
item.description:
New analysis of NHS England data for 2025 shows that the vast majority of NHS acute trusts in England are failing to meet the national cancer treatment target, with significant variation in performance across the country. The longstanding NHS standard requires that 85% of patients begin cancer treatment within 62 days of an urgent referral. However, this benchmark has not been achieved nationally since 2014. In 2025, only three out of 119 trusts with comparable data met or exceeded the 85% target: Calderdale and Huddersfield (89.2%), Homerton Healthcare (85.8%), and Maidstone and Tunbridge Wells (85.7%). Overall, just around a quarter of trusts surpassed the government’s interim goal of 75%, which is set to be reached nationally by March 2026.
Across England, 69.1% of cancer patients began treatment within 62 days in 2025, a slight improvement from 67.7% in 2024 but still well below the national target. While 65 trusts recorded year-on-year improvements, 54 saw performance decline. So

item.title: Russian Shadow Fleet Oil Tankers Continue to Transit the English Channel Despite Sanctions
item.description:
A Sky News investigation reports that dozens of tankers linked to Russia’s so-called “shadow fleet” are regularly transiting the English Channel, carrying millions of barrels of oil that help finance Moscow’s war in Ukraine. These vessels, often operating under opaque ownership structures and frequently changing flags, move Russian crude from Baltic ports toward buyers such as China and India. Despite Western sanctions, embargoes and price caps, the ships continue to pass through one of the world’s busiest shipping lanes with limited direct interference.
During a February voyage in the Dover Strait, journalists tracked three sanctioned tankers — the Rigel, the Hyperion and the Kousai — each carrying oil worth tens of millions of dollars. While the UK and EU have sanctioned many such vessels, enforcement in international waters is legally complex. Under maritime law, ships are entitled to innocent passage if properly flagged, limiting the grounds for detention. British authorities have

item.title: Peter Mandelson arrested amid investigation but denies flight risk claims
item.description: Former Labour peer Peter Mandelson, 72, was arrested by the Metropolitan Police on Monday under suspicion of misconduct in public office but was released the same evening. The arrest followed claims that he was a flight risk, allegedly planning to leave the UK for the British Virgin Islands. Mandelson's lawyers, Mishcon de Rey, called these claims 'baseless' and emphasized that he had agreed to attend a voluntary police interview next month. The police decided to arrest him earlier due to concerns stemming from time-consuming searches of his homes in Camden, north London, and Wiltshire, and subsequent interviews regarding the flight risk claims. Mandelson has consistently denied any wrongdoing and aims to cooperate fully with the investigation to clear his name. He was bailed after about eight hours of questioning, though it is not confirmed whether he had to surrender his passport, which is common in such cases. Mandelson previously served as British ambassador to the US but was dismi

item.title: War Losses, Falling Birthrates and Migration Deepen Ukraine’s Demographic Crisis
item.description:
As the war with Russia approaches its fourth year, Ukraine is facing a severe demographic crisis marked by heavy military casualties, plummeting birth rates, and mass migration. According to leading demographer Ella Libanova, the country has lost around 10 million people since the conflict began, due to deaths, displacement, and occupation. Fertility rates have dropped below one child per woman, significantly lower than the European and US averages, worsening a decline that had already begun before the war.
The human toll of the conflict is visible in stories like that of Olena Bilozerska, a former soldier who delayed motherhood to serve on the frontlines. After struggling with fertility issues and undergoing in vitro fertilization, her embryo remained frozen in Kyiv as Russia launched its full-scale invasion. She eventually gave birth at 46, but her experience reflects the difficult choices many Ukrainian women face between military service and family life.
Medical professionals at

item.title: Disabled Woman Moved to Nursing Home Against Her Will Raises Concerns About NHS Care Decisions
item.description: Lucinda Ritchie, a 33-year-old disabled woman from Billingshurst, West Sussex, has spoken out after being moved to a nursing home against her will. Previously living independently in her adapted bungalow with 24-hour one-to-one nursing care, she was hospitalized last April due to pneumonia. Ten months later, instead of returning home, she was transferred to Temple Grove Nursing Home in Uckfield, an hour away from her family. Within two days, her health deteriorated, requiring a hospital return. Ritchie, who has Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, functional neurological disorder, Addison's disease, epilepsy, and requires a tracheostomy and ventilator support, communicates primarily through eye-gaze technology. Her mother and legal team argued that it was safe for her to return home, but NHS Sussex did not provide meaningful engagement or care planning for a home return. Legal experts noted that moving an adult with full mental capacity to a residential home against their will is unusual and potent

item.title: NHS obtains alternative bone cement to continue joint surgeries
item.description: The NHS has successfully secured a supply of alternative bone cement, allowing postponed joint surgeries, including hip and knee replacements, to resume. Last week, hospitals were instructed to delay non-urgent procedures after a major supplier, Heraeus Medical, experienced a critical machine failure at its German facility, affecting around three-quarters of the NHS's bone cement supply. The disruption was expected to last for two months, raising concerns for 850,000 patients currently on waiting lists for planned joint treatments. To address the shortfall, the NHS has obtained a 10 to 12-week supply from Dutch manufacturer Zimmer Biomet, and existing supplier Johnson & Johnson will also increase deliveries. The alternative cement has been safely used since 2008 and has been confirmed by surgical experts to have identical chemical and material properties, ensuring that patient outcomes will not be affected. Health officials emphasized that the immediate focus is to resume postponed sur

item.title: UK Introduces Mandatory Electronic Travel Authorisation System, Creating Challenges for Dual Nationals
item.description:
The UK has introduced a new Electronic Travel Authorisation (ETA) system that will become mandatory from 25 February for visitors from 85 countries who previously did not require a visa for short stays. The ETA is a digital permission linked to a traveller’s passport and allows multiple visits of up to six months over a two-year period, or until the passport expires. It applies to travel for tourism, business, short-term study, or transit through the UK, although passengers who remain airside and do not pass through border control are exempt. Those who already require a visa will still need one and do not need to apply for an ETA.
The application costs £16, with plans to raise the fee to £20. Applicants must provide passport and contact details, upload a suitable photo, and answer security questions, typically receiving a decision within minutes through a mobile app. However, travellers are advised to apply at least three working days before departure. If an ETA is refused, there is n

item.title: Nearly a Quarter of English Councils Unable to Meet Weekly Food Waste Collection Deadline
item.description: A BBC investigation has found that almost a quarter of councils in England will not meet the upcoming government deadline to provide weekly food waste collections to all households. The Department for Environment, Food & Rural Affairs (Defra) mandated weekly collections under new legislation, aiming to standardize recycling across the country. Despite more than £340 million in government grants and increased budgets, at least 71 councils reported they would miss the March deadline. Delays are attributed to high demand for specialized collection vehicles and insufficient revenue funding for running weekly services. Some councils, like Shropshire and East Hampshire, face logistical challenges, while others, including Worcester, plan rollouts as late as 2027. The Local Government Association has requested more clarity on funding and flexibility in service delivery. The policy, known as Simpler Recycling, seeks to remove the 'postcode lottery' of differing local recycling rules, ensuring a

item.title: Mexico Urged to Develop a National AI Strategy to Strengthen Economic Growth
item.description: Centro México Digital has released a comprehensive study titled “Artificial Intelligence in Mexico: From Promise to Economic Impact,” analyzing the effects of AI adoption on Mexican businesses using data from the 2024 Economic Census by INEGI, covering 5.2 million companies. The study highlights that AI adoption is a critical driver of economic performance, showing that a 10% increase in AI adoption correlates with a 5.2% increase in gross production and a 3.8% rise in average wages. However, the impact on employment varies across companies, aligning with international research on AI’s labor effects. Compared internationally, Mexico remains in the early stages of AI adoption, with only 8% of companies with ten or more employees using AI, compared to an OECD average of 20.2%. Large Mexican companies adopt AI at 17%, while small businesses lag at 6%, revealing a 2.8x adoption gap. Geographic disparities are also evident, with states like Nuevo León, Tamaulipas, and Mexico City leading ad

item.title: South Africans return home after being misled into fighting in Ukraine
item.description: South African President Cyril Ramaphosa has expressed gratitude to Russian President Vladimir Putin for assisting in the return of 17 South Africans who were allegedly misled into joining the Russia-Ukraine war. The men had been promised bodyguard training in Russia but were instead placed on the frontlines in Ukraine. The South African government confirmed that four men had already returned, 11 more were on their way home, and two others would return later. Authorities are investigating the recruitment process. Working as a mercenary without government authorization is illegal in South Africa. Relatives reported that the men signed contracts written in Russian that they could not understand, which committed them to fight in the conflict. Duduzile Zuma-Sambudla, daughter of former President Jacob Zuma, has been accused of recruiting the men, though she denies wrongdoing and claims she believed the training was lawful. Following these events, Zuma-Sambudla resigned as a member of parlia

item.title: West Bromwich Albion Sack Head Coach Eric Ramsay After Nine Winless Games Amid Relegation Battle
item.description: West Bromwich Albion have parted ways with head coach Eric Ramsay after a brief and unsuccessful tenure of just nine games. The 34-year-old Ramsay, who previously worked as a coach at Manchester United and most recently with Minnesota United in MLS, was appointed on January 11, 2026, on a two-and-a-half-year contract to replace the sacked Ryan Mason. At the time of his arrival, West Brom sat 18th in the Championship table, seven points clear of the relegation zone. However, under Ramsay's leadership, the team failed to secure a single victory, collecting only a handful of draws and losses that saw them slide perilously close to the drop. The final match under his charge was a 1-1 home draw against Charlton Athletic, which left Albion just one point above the relegation places. The club announced the decision in an official statement, confirming that assistant head coach Dennis Lawrence has also departed, while first-team coach James Morrison will take interim charge of the first team.

item.title: Peter Attia Resigns as CBS News Contributor Following Release of Epstein-Related Emails
item.description: Dr. Peter Attia, a prominent physician and influencer in longevity and anti-aging medicine, has stepped down from his recently appointed role as a CBS News contributor. The decision follows the public release of documents by the US Department of Justice that included email exchanges between Attia and the late convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein. These emails, dating from 2014 to 2019, contained personal and sometimes crude remarks, including crass comments about women's bodies and expressions of missing Epstein's company. Attia visited Epstein's New York home several times but has repeatedly stated he witnessed no illegal activities, was never on Epstein's plane or island, and had no involvement in any criminal acts or sex trafficking. His spokesman emphasized that the CBS role was new and had barely started, and Attia chose to step back to prevent becoming a distraction for the network. Attia was one of several experts appointed in January 2026 by CBS News editor-in-chief Bari Weis

item.title: Nigeria rejects claims of ransom payment in St Mary's school abduction
item.description: The Nigerian government has strongly denied claims by AFP that it paid a large ransom to Boko Haram for the release of over 200 pupils and staff abducted from St Mary's School in Papiri, Niger State, in November. Information Minister Mohammed Idris called the report 'completely false and baseless', emphasizing that it undermines the integrity and professionalism of Nigeria's security forces. Allegations that two Boko Haram commanders were released or that ransom payments of 40 million naira per person were delivered by helicopter were also dismissed as fiction. The Department of State Services (DSS) described the claims as fake. All pupils and staff were reportedly rescued safely by security forces in December. Nigeria faces widespread security challenges including kidnappings for ransom carried out by Boko Haram, criminal gangs, and separatists. While ransom payments remain a concern, a 2022 law criminalized such payments, though enforcement is challenging. The controversy coincides w

item.title: Inquiry reveals police failures in arresting Nottingham triple killer before attacks
item.description: An inquiry has revealed serious lapses by Nottinghamshire and Leicestershire police in handling Valdo Calocane, who killed three people in Nottingham in June 2023 and injured three others. An arrest warrant for Calocane had been issued in September 2022, over nine months before the attacks, after he failed to attend a court hearing for allegedly assaulting an emergency worker. Despite multiple incidents, including assaults on colleagues and a university flatmate, police failed to execute the warrant. Nottinghamshire Police described their management of Calocane as mostly handling minor antisocial behavior incidents, and noted that officers are not trained to manage paranoid schizophrenia, emphasizing the role of mental health services instead. Legal representatives of victims criticized any suggestion that arresting Calocane earlier would have made no difference as offensive. Leicestershire Police also acknowledged failings when attending a prior assault incident, including officers no

item.title: Houston Cougars Favored Against Kansas Jayhawks in Big 12 Matchup
item.description: The Houston Cougars are set to face the Kansas Jayhawks in Lawrence, KS, with tip-off scheduled for 9 p.m. ET on ESPN. Houston is slightly favored by 2 points and has a moneyline of -135, while the game total is projected at 138 points. Houston seeks its fourth consecutive No. 1 seed in the NCAA Tournament, leveraging a strong roster led by five-star recruits Chris Cenac Jr., Isiah Harwell, and Kingston Flemings. Flemings has been particularly impactful, averaging 17 points, five assists, and over three rebounds per game. Emanuel Sharp has also emerged as a key contributor, averaging a career-high 16 points per game. Under Kelvin Sampson, Houston boasts top-10 efficiency on both offense and defense, with a focus on physical play and offensive rebounding. Kansas enters the game with a 20-7 record but recently lost to Cincinnati at home, highlighting some inconsistency. Star freshman Darryn Peterson, a likely No. 1 NBA draft pick, has been selectively playing this season, contributing 20

item.title: Powerful Nor’easter Leaves Two Dead and Causes Major Disruptions Across US Northeast
item.description:
A powerful winter storm swept across the northeastern United States, leaving at least two people dead and causing widespread disruption across several states. In Calvert County, Maryland, a tragic accident occurred when a falling tree struck a vehicle, killing two occupants, identified as Michael Simpson, 60, and Virginia Quesenberry, 43. Authorities reported that heavy snowfall and strong winds contributed to hazardous road conditions throughout the region.
Maryland experienced snowfall totals of up to 16 inches (40 cm), while Providence, Rhode Island, recorded 37.9 inches, marking the largest blizzard in the city’s history. New York City faced its ninth-largest winter storm on record, with more than 20 inches of snow. In response, New York City officials implemented a travel ban to minimize accidents and allow sanitation crews to clear roads. Thousands of workers were deployed to plow streets and shovel sidewalks.
The storm severely disrupted air travel, with more than 5,000 flight

item.title: Christian Horner Denies Verstappen Family Role in His Red Bull Departure
item.description:
Christian Horner has publicly addressed the circumstances surrounding his departure from Red Bull in 2025, firmly denying that Max Verstappen or his family played any role in his exit. Speaking to Netflix’s Formula 1: Drive to Survive, Horner stated that although Max Verstappen’s father, Jos Verstappen, had been openly critical of him in the past, he does not believe the Verstappen camp was responsible for the decision to remove him from his positions as team principal and chief executive.
Horner left Red Bull after an 18-month period marked by controversy and internal instability. The turmoil began in February 2024 when a female employee accused him of sexual harassment. Although Horner was twice cleared of the allegations—first through an internal investigation and later through an appeal review—the situation contributed to ongoing tensions within the organization. During this period, Red Bull also experienced a decline in performance and internal disputes at the leadership level.

item.title: European Leaders Mark Fourth Anniversary of Ukraine War as Prospects for Peace Remain Distant
item.description:
On the fourth anniversary of Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, European leaders gathered in Kyiv in a show of solidarity with President Volodymyr Zelenskyy and the Ukrainian people. The ceremony in Maidan Square included a minute’s silence to honor the tens of thousands of Ukrainian soldiers killed since the war began. Despite the symbolic unity, there was a shared recognition among attendees that the conflict is far from over and may continue for years.
UK Foreign Secretary Yvette Cooper used the visit to emphasize not only military and economic support for Ukraine but also concerns over what she described as Russia’s broader campaign against Ukrainian culture and identity. She highlighted allegations that thousands of Ukrainian children have been forcibly taken to Russia, where they are reportedly subjected to re-education and indoctrination. Cooper also met with victims of sexual violence and civil rights activists who accused the Kremlin of pursuing genocidal intentions by attempting

item.title: From Gutenberg’s Printing Press to Generative AI: The Dialectics of Knowledge and the Revival of Orality
item.description:
The article reflects on the historical transformation of knowledge transmission, drawing a parallel between the invention of Gutenberg’s movable-type printing press in the 15th century and the current rise of generative artificial intelligence. In medieval Europe and many earlier cultures, knowledge was primarily oral, conveyed through sermons, troubadours, university debates, and monastic readings. Memory and rhetoric were central intellectual skills, and learning was inseparable from communal listening and recitation.
With the arrival of the printing press, knowledge became more accessible and affordable, but this technological shift also generated anxiety. Some humanists feared that mass-produced books would weaken memory, encourage intellectual laziness, and flood society with low-quality texts. Printed knowledge separated information from the authority of the individual teacher and transformed it into a commodity. Despite these concerns, the printing revolution did not destroy or

item.title: Ukrainian Former Wall Street Professional Returns Home as Combat Medic Amid Russia-Ukraine War
item.description: Viktoriia Honcharuk, a Ukrainian national, gave up a promising career on Wall Street to serve as a combat medic in her home country after Russia launched its full-scale invasion in February 2022. Despite her initial fear of blood and needles, she felt compelled to return to Ukraine after witnessing her family and fellow citizens join the resistance. Viktoriia described the decision as 'the easiest decision' she ever made, motivated by a sense of duty and solidarity with her homeland. She quickly underwent a few days of medical training before being deployed to the frontlines, where she treats wounded soldiers from both sides, including Russian prisoners of war, in accordance with the Geneva Convention. Viktoriia emphasizes that the resilience and courage of ordinary Ukrainians have been crucial in resisting Russian aggression, noting that their collective refusal to stand aside has helped protect Ukraine from further domination. Her story highlights the personal sacrifices and moral co

item.title: Heavy rains in Minas Gerais, Brazil cause floods and landslides killing 20
item.description: At least 20 people have died in the south-eastern Brazilian state of Minas Gerais after heavy rains on Monday evening triggered severe floods and multiple landslides. The city of Juiz de Fora reported the majority of fatalities, with 15 deaths, while Ubá accounted for another six. Rescue operations are ongoing as authorities search for dozens of missing residents following the collapse of homes and buildings. Brazilian President Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva expressed condolences to affected families and declared a state of calamity in Juiz de Fora, pledging government support for humanitarian aid and reconstruction efforts. Around 440 people have been displaced, with temporary shelters set up and donations of essential supplies requested. Local officials described this as the rainiest February in the city's history, with rainfall more than double the monthly average. Social media videos have shown widespread devastation, including vehicles swept away by floodwaters and buildings collapsin

item.title: Mayweather and Pacquiao Rematch Highlights Boxing Entertainment Over Competition
item.description: Floyd Mayweather, 49, and Manny Pacquiao, 47, are set to face each other in a professional boxing rematch in Las Vegas on September 19. The fight is widely described as more of a spectacle than a competitive sporting event, with critics calling it a 'carnival' of boxing. Both fighters are considered past their prime, but their similar age and experience make the bout relatively safe compared to other exhibition-style 'freak fights' popularized by fighters like Jake Paul. The rules are yet to be fully confirmed, but the rematch will count as a professional fight on their records, with standard gloves, rounds, and no special modifications. While competitive action may be limited, the promotional aspect promises high revenue and attention, following the model established by Mayweather’s 2017 fight against UFC star Conor McGregor. The event will take place at the Sphere in Las Vegas, accommodating 20,000 spectators and potentially delivering a novel viewing experience, amplified by Netflix

item.title: Tottenham Face Relegation Battle Amid Poor Form, Injuries and Financial Concerns
item.description:
Tottenham Hotspur are facing a serious relegation battle after a heavy 4-1 defeat to Arsenal in the north London derby left them just four points above the Premier League relegation zone. Under interim head coach Igor Tudor, Spurs have struggled for consistency, failing to win a domestic league match in 2026 and managing only two victories since late October 2025. Their poor home record at Tottenham Hotspur Stadium, where they have collected just 10 points from a possible 42, has compounded their problems.
With 11 league games remaining, including crucial fixtures against fellow strugglers such as Wolves, Crystal Palace, Brighton, Nottingham Forest and Leeds, Tottenham’s survival hopes may depend on rediscovering form quickly. Opta statistics estimate their relegation risk at 4.84%, but their recent average of just 0.67 points per game suggests they could finish the season on a precarious 36 points if the slump continues.
Injuries have further complicated matters, with key attacking

item.title: Harry Brook leads England to T20 World Cup semi-final with century in Pallekele
item.description: Harry Brook delivered a standout performance for the England men's cricket team in Pallekele, scoring a brilliant 100 off 51 balls in a T20 international against Pakistan. Coming after a challenging winter marked by off-field controversies and inconsistent form, Brook's innings showcased his maturity, tactical awareness, and leadership on the field. After England lost early wickets and found themselves at 58-4 chasing 165, Brook stabilized the innings, carefully balancing aggressive shots with smart running between the wickets. His century not only secured victory but also ensured England's progression to the World Cup semi-finals. The innings reflected Brook's growth as a leader and a batsman, under the guidance of coach Brendon McCullum, who promoted him to bat at number three for the first time in his international career. Brook's strategic approach and composure in high-pressure situations highlight his potential to lead England in future tournaments, bringing tactical intelligence

item.title: Fears of Renewed Violence in Guadalajara After CJNG Rampage and Killing of El Mencho
item.description:
Guadalajara, in Mexico’s western state of Jalisco, is grappling with the aftermath of a violent rampage carried out by the Jalisco New Generation Cartel (CJNG) following the reported killing of its leader, Nemesio “El Mencho” Oseguera. Burned vehicles, scorched highways, and damaged businesses remain visible across the city, underscoring the cartel’s capacity to unleash widespread chaos. The attacks were widely interpreted as a show of force, signaling that the group remains powerful even without its alleged chief.
President Claudia Sheinbaum has sought to reassure the public, stating that calm is returning and praising the actions of security forces. Military and police patrols have increased in Guadalajara, but many residents remain uneasy. Businesses and schools temporarily closed, and streets appeared unusually quiet as people stayed home out of fear of further violence.
Local café owner Anwar Montoya chose to open despite the risks, describing the atmosphere as tense and uncerta

item.title: Deregulation of Electricity in Ohio Increased Costs Through Middlemen
item.description: Recent research from The Ohio State University’s Energy Markets and Policy Group shows that electricity deregulation in several U.S. states, including Ohio, has led to higher, not lower, prices for consumers. Between the late 1990s and early 2000s, deregulation split electricity services, allowing wholesale generation markets to compete, while transmission and distribution remained regulated. Retail consumers could choose to buy electricity through open-market suppliers or stick with default service, but most people chose the latter. Researchers found that 72% of open-market offers were more expensive than default rates, indicating that competition did not deliver the promised savings. Suppliers often set their prices based on the default service auction outcomes rather than market fundamentals. Additionally, default service auctions were found to have limited competition, and fewer bidders resulted in higher electricity rates. Adding just a few extra bidders could significantly lower

item.title: David Lammy Admits Court Backlog May Persist Despite Planned Justice Reforms
item.description:
In this episode of Newscast, Adam Fleming and Chris Mason interview Deputy Prime Minister David Lammy about the government’s proposed justice reforms and the ongoing challenges facing the UK court system. Lammy openly acknowledged that the existing court backlog is likely to continue rising and may remain at similar levels by the time of the next general election, despite planned government interventions. His remarks highlight the scale and complexity of the crisis within the justice system, which has been under strain due to years of underfunding, pandemic-related delays, and increased case volumes.
During the discussion, Lammy emphasized that the Labour government understands the urgency of reform but warned that meaningful structural change takes time. He suggested that while measures are being introduced to improve efficiency and case processing, the public should not expect immediate results. The deputy prime minister also made a politically striking comment, stating that Labour

item.title: 23 Prisoners Escape in Puerto Vallarta Amid Violence Following Death of Cartel Leader El Mencho
item.description:
Mexican authorities are searching for 23 inmates who escaped from a prison in Puerto Vallarta during a surge of violence triggered by the death of Nemesio Oseguera Cervantes, known as 'El Mencho,' leader of the Jalisco New Generation Cartel (CJNG). The unrest erupted after Mexican security forces captured El Mencho in the town of Tapalpa, Jalisco state. He later died from injuries sustained during a firefight between his guards and special forces. During the coordinated attacks that followed, cartel members blockaded roads and set vehicles on fire across Puerto Vallarta, a major tourist destination on Mexico’s Pacific coast. Armed men rammed a vehicle into a prison gate, enabling 23 prisoners to escape. Authorities have not disclosed the identities of the fugitives.
The operation to capture El Mencho and the subsequent violence resulted in more than 70 deaths, including 25 members of Mexico’s National Guard. State security officials in Jalisco are working with other regions to locate

item.title: Parliamentary Debate on Prince Andrew Signals Changing Attitudes Toward Royal Accountability
item.description:
Members of the UK Parliament have openly criticised Prince Andrew during a formal debate, marking a notable departure from longstanding conventions that traditionally shield the Royal Family from direct criticism in the House of Commons. The debate, initiated by the Liberal Democrats, called for the release of documents related to Andrew’s 2001 appointment as the UK’s trade envoy. Several MPs used strong language to describe their concerns about his conduct and questioned the oversight and transparency surrounding his decade-long tenure in the role.
The discussion also revisited allegations that Andrew shared sensitive information with convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein, whose associations with prominent public figures have sparked ongoing controversy. While Andrew has denied wrongdoing and has not responded to recent BBC requests for comment regarding newly released US files connected to Epstein, MPs argued that the public deserves greater clarity about his official duties and ho

item.title: UK Government Reopens Revised Sustainable Farming Incentive with Funding Caps and Reforms
item.description:
The UK government has announced the relaunch of its flagship environmental payment programme for farmers, the Sustainable Farming Incentive (SFI), after its abrupt closure in March 2025 caused widespread concern across the agricultural sector. Environment Secretary Emma Reynolds confirmed the scheme’s return at the National Farmers' Union (NFU) conference, stating that the revised programme aims to balance environmental protection with food production.
The SFI, which replaced EU agricultural subsidies after Brexit, pays farmers in England to carry out environmentally beneficial practices such as soil protection, hedgerow restoration and nature recovery. The updated scheme will be smaller in scope, reducing the number of funded environmental actions from 102 to 71. Individual farm agreements will also be capped at £100,000. Reynolds said these changes are intended to ensure funding is distributed more fairly, after assessments showed that 25% of funds had gone to just 4% of farms.
The

item.title: Lammy warns court backlog may keep rising despite reform package to ease pressure
item.description:
Justice Secretary David Lammy has acknowledged that the crown court backlog in England and Wales is expected to continue rising despite a new package of reforms aimed at addressing mounting delays. Currently standing at around 80,000 cases, the backlog could exceed 200,000 by 2035 if no action is taken, according to Ministry of Justice projections. Lammy said that although he hopes to see reductions by the next general election, substantial improvements are unlikely before the 2030s because the legislative changes will not take effect until late 2026 or early 2027.
The government’s proposed reforms include removing the cap on court sitting days, expanding the use of artificial intelligence to handle administrative tasks such as transcribing hearings, and introducing so-called “Blitz courts” that group similar cases together to improve efficiency. The plan also involves scrapping jury trials for certain offences likely to result in sentences of less than three years, following recommen

item.title: Man pleads guilty to theft of £2m Fabergé egg, watch and fraud charges in London
item.description:
A 29-year-old man has admitted in court to stealing a handbag containing a Fabergé egg and a watch believed to be worth more than £2 million. Enzo Conticello, who is also known as Hakin Boudjenoune and is listed as having no fixed address, pleaded guilty at Southwark Crown Court to theft and three counts of fraud by misrepresentation.
The theft took place in Soho, central London, on 7 November 2024. According to prosecutors, the stolen handbag not only contained the valuable Fabergé egg and luxury watch, but also a laptop and credit cards. After taking the bag, Conticello used the stolen credit cards to buy cigarettes and other items at supermarkets in the Soho area.
The court heard that the Fabergé egg, described as a highly valuable and rare item, has not yet been recovered. Fabergé is an internationally renowned jeweller founded in Russia over two centuries ago, famous for crafting intricate eggs made from precious metals and gemstones. The disappearance of such an item has raised

item.title: Court hears allegations of prolonged domestic abuse preceding wife's suicide
item.description: Christopher Trybus, from Swindon, Wiltshire, is on trial at Winchester Crown Court accused of the manslaughter of his wife, Tarryn Baird, who died at the age of 34 in November 2017. The prosecution alleges that Trybus engaged in sustained coercive and controlling behavior, including physical and sexual violence, which severely affected Baird's mental health and contributed to her decision to take her own life. Charges include controlling and coercive behavior and two counts of rape. Prosecutor Tom Little KC described a pattern of escalating abuse over the course of the marriage, including monitoring Baird's movements, limiting her access to finances, isolating her from family, and threatening violence. Baird, originally from South Africa and diagnosed with possible PTSD, had previously attempted to seek help from Swindon Women's Aid and her GP, and had considered leaving her husband but felt unable to do so due to fear. In September 2017, she made a previous suicide attempt, citing a d

item.title: Snoop Dogg Performs Private Set at Swansea Business Ahead of First Visit as Swansea City Co-Owner
item.description:
American rapper Snoop Dogg surprised staff at the Au Vodka headquarters in Swansea by performing a private, invitation-only concert in the company’s canteen. The event took place ahead of his first official visit to Swansea City’s home stadium since becoming a minority co-owner of the football club. Originally expected to deliver a short DJ set, Snoop instead performed an hour-long show featuring many of his best-known hits, transforming the staff canteen—where employees had been playing table tennis and pool the day before—into a nightclub-style venue with more than 400 guests.
The event was organized at short notice, with nearly all company staff in attendance alongside friends, family members, local influencers, and notable guests including boxer Joe Cordina and former Swansea striker Lee Trundle. The appearance was facilitated through business connections involving DJ Charlie Sloth, who has ties within the hip-hop industry.
Snoop Dogg’s visit to Swansea also marks a significant m

item.title: Two British Tourists Charged After Valentine's Day Stabbing in Venice
item.description:
Two British cousins, aged 19 and 21, have been arrested and charged with attempted murder following a stabbing incident in Venice on Valentine’s Day. According to Italian authorities, the attack took place on the evening of 14 February outside La Bussola Cocktail Bar, located near the Rialto Bridge in the city’s historic centre. The victim, a 22-year-old local man, was allegedly stabbed in the neck during what police described as a dispute stemming from trivial disagreements. Reports indicate that the victim came close to bleeding to death as a result of the injuries.
Italy’s Polizia di Stato stated that the 19-year-old is suspected of carrying out the stabbing, while the 21-year-old is believed to have acted as an accomplice. Investigators think the weapon used in the attack may have been thrown into the Grand Canal, prompting state police diving units to search the water in an effort to recover the knife.
The two suspects had reportedly been in Italy on holiday since late January a

item.title: Noah B. Taylor Exits ‘Wednesday’ as Joanna Lumley Becomes Series Regular for Season 3
item.description:
Netflix’s “Wednesday” is undergoing notable cast changes ahead of its third season. Noah B. Taylor, who joined the series in Season 2 as Bruno Yuson, will not return as a series regular. His character, introduced as a romantic interest for Enid Sinclair, exits the story following the collapse of their relationship. In the Season 2 storyline, Enid discovers that Bruno had maintained ties with his ex-girlfriend, leading to their breakup. Bruno is last seen leaving Nevermore Academy with his former partner, effectively closing his arc.
Meanwhile, Joanna Lumley has been promoted to series regular for Season 3. Lumley portrays Hester Frump, also known as Granmama, Wednesday’s wealthy and formidable maternal grandmother. Introduced in Season 2, Hester has a tense relationship with her daughter Morticia Addams. The Season 2 finale hints at a larger storyline involving Hester, who is revealed to be keeping her long-missing daughter Ophelia imprisoned in her mansion’s basement. Ophelia, previo

item.title: Lorry Driver Pleads Guilty After Mother Dies From Falling Crane Equipment
item.description: A 71-year-old lorry driver, Kevin Miller from King's Lynn, Norfolk, has admitted causing the death of 30-year-old Rebecca Ableman, who was fatally injured by loose crane equipment while pushing her two-year-old daughter Autumn in Willingham, Cambridgeshire. The incident occurred on 22 September 2022, when Miller was transporting scrap metal between Network Rail depots in Essex and Cambridgeshire. As he passed through Willingham, unsecured crane equipment on his trailer shifted and struck Ms Ableman on the head, causing head and brain injuries. She succumbed to her injuries three weeks later. Miller initially denied causing death by dangerous driving, and his trial began last year, but it was halted in April 2025, with a new trial scheduled. He claimed he was unaware of the accident until his arrest. Sentencing is set for 14 April at Peterborough Crown Court. The case highlights issues around vehicle safety, secure transport of heavy equipment, and the tragic consequences of carelessnes

item.title: Rose Wylie Becomes the First Female British Painter to Hold a Solo Exhibition in the Royal Academy’s Main Galleries
item.description:
At 91, British artist Rose Wylie is preparing to make history as the first female British painter to hold a solo exhibition in the main galleries of London’s Royal Academy. Known for her bold, playful and unconventional style, Wylie continues to work with the energy of a much younger artist, often painting late into the night in her Kent studio. Her creative process is intense and sometimes frustrating, involving scraping off paint and reworking canvases until she achieves what she calls a “poetic transformation” rather than a literal copy of her subjects.
The exhibition, titled “Rose Wylie: The Picture Comes First,” features 90 works spanning different inspirations, from domestic scenes and neighboring houses to footballers such as Wayne Rooney and Thierry Henry, and film references including Quentin Tarantino’s “Kill Bill.” Wylie draws on diverse sources such as newspaper images, advertisements, classical painting, and personal memories. Her work frequently shifts perspective, turni

item.title: Ukraine Commemorates War Dead as Conflict Continues into Fifth Year
item.description: Four years after Russia launched its full-scale invasion of Ukraine, the conflict shows no signs of ending. Ukraine continues to defend its territory, particularly in the eastern Donbas region, despite mounting casualties on both sides. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky emphasized the nation's resilience and sovereignty, asserting that Ukraine has defended its independence. Ceremonies across Kyiv and towns like Bucha honored fallen soldiers, highlighting the widespread human toll of the war. International support remains strong, with European leaders, including Finland's Alexander Stubb, Sweden's Ulf Kristersson, and European Commission President Ursula Von der Leyen, showing solidarity. A Coalition of the Willing, led by the UK and France, reinforced this support, though peace negotiations with Russia remain stalled. UK Prime Minister Sir Keir Starmer criticized Russian leadership as the main obstacle, while French President Emmanuel Macron expressed skepticism about achieving sh

item.title: Lord Mandelson Arrested by Police Amid Misconduct Investigation, Lawyers Deny Flight Risk
item.description: Lord Peter Mandelson was arrested at his London home on Monday by the Metropolitan Police on suspicion of misconduct in public office, according to his legal team. His lawyers emphasized that the arrest was prompted by unverified concerns that he might leave the UK, a claim they firmly denied. Despite an agreement to voluntarily attend an interview with police next month, the arrest went ahead, with his lawyers stating that Mandelson has consistently cooperated with the ongoing investigation and intends to continue doing so. A spokesperson for Mishcon De Reya, representing Mandelson, has requested that the Metropolitan Police provide the evidence that justified the arrest. The police have refrained from commenting on the case. The news story is developing, and further updates are expected as the investigation continues. Mandelson’s legal team is focused on clearing his name while cooperating fully with authorities.

item.title: Twin sisters discover they are identical after one is diagnosed with leukaemia
item.description: Caitlin and Grace Leggett, twin sisters from Cardiff, believed they were fraternal twins until a medical emergency revealed otherwise. At age 23, Caitlin was diagnosed with acute myeloid leukaemia (AML), a fast-progressing cancer affecting white blood cells. As part of her treatment, she required a stem cell transplant, and initially, her sister Grace was considered a potential donor due to their close genetic relation. However, doctors discovered that the sisters were in fact identical twins, meaning Grace's stem cells could not be used because a successful transplant requires the donor's cells to be recognized as foreign by the recipient's body to attack remaining cancer cells. This discovery complicated Caitlin's treatment, leading to her participation in a clinical trial in Manchester and additional full-body radiotherapy. Eventually, Caitlin successfully received a transplant from an anonymous donor. The sisters are now advocating for more people to register as stem cell donors, e

item.title: Reform UK suspends campaign manager over racist and antisemitic social media posts
item.description: Reform UK has suspended Adam Mitula, the interim campaign manager for the Gorton and Denton by-election, after offensive comments he made on social media were revealed. The remarks, which reportedly included a racial slur directed at black people and a derogatory comment about Jewish women, were flagged by the anti-racism group Hope Not Hate. Mitula also appeared to agree with a Holocaust denier suggesting the number of Jewish victims during World War II had been exaggerated. Mitula has stated that his comments were taken out of context and that he was actually defending the Jewish community. He provided no comment regarding the racial slur. Reform UK emphasized that its disciplinary process is confidential and declined to give detailed updates on ongoing cases. The by-election affects over 76,000 potential voters across Denton in Tameside, and Gorton, Levenshulme, and Burnage in Manchester. This suspension occurs amid growing scrutiny of political figures' social media activity and it

item.title: Authorities Investigate Canine Distemper Virus Behind Tiger Deaths in Thailand
item.description: In February 2026, 72 tigers died at two tourist parks, Mae Taeng and Mae Rim, in Chiang Mai province, Thailand. Officials have confirmed that the deaths were caused by a virus not known to infect humans, specifically the canine distemper virus (CDV), though bacterial infections were also detected in autopsies. Veterinary expert Visit Arsaithamkul raised concerns about the unclear origins of the infection, suggesting the tigers may have contracted it through contaminated food from a shared source. Thai authorities emphasize that there has been no evidence of animal-to-human transmission, but public health monitoring continues for individuals who had contact with the animals. Both parks, operated under the name Tiger Kingdom, remain closed, and the remains of the tigers were cremated or buried following strict disinfection protocols. CDV is particularly dangerous for felines and tigers in confined environments, especially under stress or inbred conditions. The outbreak sparked initial fe

item.title: Inquiry Finds Police Failed to Execute Warrant Before Triple Killing in Nottingham
item.description: A judge-led inquiry has revealed serious failings by Nottinghamshire Police in the months leading up to the fatal stabbing of three people and the injury of three others by Valdo Calocane on 13 June 2023. Calocane had an outstanding arrest warrant issued 10 months prior after failing to attend court for assaulting a police officer in 2021. The warrant was considered a 'low priority,' and additional allegations of assault against colleagues at a factory went unaddressed. Nottinghamshire Police have acknowledged a 'serious, systemic and operational failure' in their handling of the case. Lawyers representing the bereaved families argue that suggestions Calocane may not have been prosecuted if arrested are offensive and dismissive of the risk to public safety. Evidence also emerged that some police calls and records from the morning of the attacks were unavailable, raising concerns about procedural oversight. Calocane, who had mental health issues, was later sentenced to a hospital order

item.title: Olaplex Relaunches No.3Plus Complete Repair Treatment with New Formula
item.description: Olaplex has relaunched its iconic No.3 hair treatment under the name No.3Plus Complete Repair Treatment, featuring a new Damage Defense Cationic Complex. This updated formula is designed to deliver immediate improvements in hair health and texture after a single use while also offering long-term protection. The relaunch replaces the original No.3 in Olaplex's distribution network and reflects the brand’s ongoing strategy to maintain growth amid heightened competition. CEO Amanda Baldwin emphasized that the No.3 product was key to Olaplex's success with consumers and represents the brand’s commitment to innovation and quality. Alongside the new formula, Olaplex has developed enhanced educational resources for consumers to simplify understanding of its scientific approach. The brand will also offer a back bar size for professional use. Accompanying the relaunch is a marketing campaign titled “Science Never Looked So Good,” featuring Olaplex’s chief hair officer Chloe Fineman, aimed at ma

item.title: UK MPs Order Release of Government Files on Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor’s Trade Envoy Appointment
item.description:
Members of Parliament have approved a motion compelling the UK government to release official documents related to Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor’s appointment as a trade envoy in 2001. The Liberal Democrats used a parliamentary mechanism known as a humble address to force ministers to disclose files, including any vetting documents associated with his appointment. The government supported the motion, with Trade Minister Sir Chris Bryant confirming that it would comply as quickly as possible, while cautioning that an ongoing police investigation could delay the process.
Andrew, who served as special representative for international trade and investment from 2001 to 2011, was recently released under investigation following his arrest on suspicion of misconduct in public office. He has strongly denied any wrongdoing. Allegations under consideration by police include claims linked to his association with the late convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein and accusations that he shared confident

item.title: England introduces same-day appointments for urgent GP cases
item.description: The UK government has announced a new contractual requirement for General Practitioners (GPs) in England to provide same-day appointments for patients with urgent health needs. From April, GP practices will be obligated to monitor and ensure that at least 90% of patients requiring urgent attention are seen the same day, with a 10% allowance for patients unable to attend. This measure is supported by additional funding equivalent to a 3.6% increase above inflation, aiming to help manage demand amid stretched services. Health Secretary Wes Streeting stated the move aims to 'fix the front door to the NHS' by improving access for urgent cases. The British Medical Association (BMA) has expressed concern that the policy could create unrealistic expectations, noting that while GP numbers have increased, patient loads remain high, and service improvement perception among patients is limited. The BMA also highlighted that they were not consulted on the changes. Patient watchdog Healthwatch Engl

item.title: Lammy announces expanded AI use in courts alongside plans to limit some jury trials
item.description:
UK Justice Secretary and Deputy Prime Minister David Lammy has announced plans to expand the use of artificial intelligence across courts in England and Wales, while also moving forward with proposals to reduce the number of jury trials in an effort to tackle the growing case backlog. Speaking at a Microsoft AI event in London, Lammy confirmed that members of the judiciary are already using AI tools and said he wants to see broader adoption through increased investment in the Ministry of Justice’s in-house 'justice AI' unit.
According to Lammy, AI has already been trialled in the probation system, where it has transcribed more than 150,000 meetings between offenders and probation officers, saving an estimated 25,000 hours of administrative time. Similar transcription technology is being tested in courts and tribunals. In the immigration and asylum chamber, some judges are using AI tools to help draft notes and remarks. Pilot schemes are also underway to assist legal advisers and distr

item.title: Lorraine Thorpe Denied Parole but Recommended for Transfer to Open Prison
item.description:
Lorraine Thorpe, believed to be Britain’s youngest female double murderer, has been approved for transfer to an open prison despite being refused parole for a second time. Thorpe, now 31, was 15 years old when she killed her father, Desmond Thorpe, 43, and Rosalyn Hunt in Ipswich in August 2009. In 2010, she was sentenced to life imprisonment with a minimum term of 14 years. Her accomplice, Paul Clarke, who was 41 at the time of the murders, received a minimum term of 27 years but died in prison four years later.
The crimes involved prolonged violence. Thorpe and Clarke tortured Ms Hunt over several days before killing her, and later smothered Thorpe’s father. Their bodies were discovered in separate flats. During sentencing at the Old Bailey, the judge described Thorpe as manipulative and stated she appeared to find violence entertaining. Although he acknowledged her troubled upbringing—living in squalid conditions and associating with older alcoholics—he concluded she was not entire

item.title: Investigation into Alice Webb’s Death Following Non-Surgical BBL Closes After Suspect Dies
item.description:
An investigation into the death of 33-year-old Alice Webb, a mother of five from Gloucestershire, has been formally closed after the death of the main suspect. Webb died on 24 September 2024 after becoming unwell following a non-surgical Brazilian butt lift (BBL) procedure. She was later pronounced dead at Gloucestershire Royal Hospital. Gloucestershire Police had arrested 34-year-old Jordan James Parke, widely known as the 'British Lip King,' on suspicion of manslaughter in connection with her death. However, he was released on police bail and had not been formally charged. Authorities have now confirmed that Parke died on Wednesday, leading to the closure of the criminal investigation as no charges can be brought against a deceased individual.
Police stated that inquiries will continue on behalf of the coroner, and inquests will be held into both Webb’s and Parke’s deaths to establish the full circumstances surrounding each case. A second individual, a woman arrested on suspicion of

item.title: Dutch Prime Minister-designate Rob Jetten reaffirms past criticisms of Donald Trump ahead of inauguration
item.description: Rob Jetten, leader of the Dutch liberal party D66 and the incoming Prime Minister of the Netherlands set to be sworn in shortly after February 21, 2026, has confirmed he stands by his previous strong criticisms of U.S. President Donald Trump. In past statements, particularly around Trump's 2024 re-election, Jetten described Trump as a 'convicted criminal' referring to U.S. court rulings, a 'misogynist,' someone who 'openly flirts with dictators,' and a 'danger to international security and the global economy.' He refused to congratulate Trump on his victory at the time. In a recent interview with RTL Nieuws, Jetten said he would not retract these remarks, arguing that true friends can be critical of each other when values are at stake. He noted that diplomatic discussions would occur upon meeting Trump in person, though a more measured tone might be necessary in his new role. The article highlights a broader shift in European views toward the U.S. under Trump, with growing doubts about

item.title: Temperatures in the UK expected to reach 18°C on Wednesday amid mild Atlantic winds
item.description: The UK is experiencing an early taste of spring as temperatures rise due to mild south-westerly winds from the Atlantic. Since last weekend, each home nation has recorded its highest temperature of the year so far. Tuesday marked the warmest day, but Wednesday is expected to see temperatures reaching 17-18°C (63-64°F) in eastern England thanks to increased sunshine. Northern and western regions will have more clouds and occasional rain, but temperatures will still be above average. Historically, the last time the UK recorded 18°C was on 13 November 2025, while the February record remains 21.2°C (70.2°F) set on 26 February 2019 at Kew Gardens. Despite the warmer weather, rainfall has not completely stopped, and areas with already saturated ground remain at risk of flooding. Western Scotland and other hilly areas are expected to see more rain, whereas eastern England will remain relatively dry. As the UK moves further into spring, low-pressure systems will continue to bring occasional ra

item.title: Christopher Trybus Denies Manslaughter Charges Over Wife’s Death Amid Allegations of Coercive Control
item.description:
A court has heard that Christopher Trybus, 43, from Swindon, Wiltshire, is accused of causing the death of his wife, Tarryn Baird, through sustained coercive and controlling behaviour. Prosecutors allege that his actions, which included physical violence, sexual violence, threats, and manipulation, led to Baird taking her own life in November 2017 at the age of 34. The case is being heard at Winchester Crown Court.
Tom Little KC, prosecuting, told the jury that Trybus subjected his wife to extensive and escalating abuse throughout their marriage. The alleged behaviour included two counts of rape, sexual assaults, monitoring her movements, restricting her financial access, isolating her from family, and threatening to reveal private information. According to the prosecution, this pattern of abuse took place behind closed doors over a prolonged period and contributed significantly to Baird’s deteriorating mental health.
The court heard that Baird left a note for her family referring to

item.title: Savannah Guthrie’s Family Announces $1m Reward in Search for Missing 84-Year-Old Mother
item.description:
Savannah Guthrie has announced that her family is offering a $1m reward for information leading to the return of her 84-year-old mother, Nancy Guthrie, who has been missing for 24 days. Speaking through tears in an Instagram video, the NBC host said the family remains hopeful for a miracle while acknowledging the possibility that her mother may no longer be alive. In addition to the reward, the family pledged to donate $500,000 to the National Center for Missing and Exploited Children.
Nancy Guthrie was reported missing on 1 February after she failed to attend church services in Tucson, Arizona. Authorities from the Pima County Sheriff’s Office believe she may have been abducted from her home during the night. Investigators released images of a prime suspect captured on her Nest doorbell camera, who had reportedly visited her home on a previous occasion. DNA evidence collected from a glove found near the property did not match any records in the FBI database.
The FBI’s Phoenix offic

item.title: BBC Apologises for Airing Racial Slur Due to Tourette's Tic at Baftas, Reveals Another Slur Was Edited Out
item.description: During the 2026 Bafta Film Awards broadcast on BBC, a racial slur (the N-word) was inadvertently aired when Tourette's syndrome campaigner John Davidson shouted it involuntarily as actors Michael B. Jordan and Delroy Lindo presented an award on stage. The ceremony was transmitted on BBC One with a two-hour delay and edited to fit a two-hour slot, yet this particular outburst was not removed due to producers in a TV truck not hearing it. BBC chief content officer Kate Phillips confirmed in an internal email that the broadcaster takes full responsibility, describing the airing as a genuine error they would never have allowed intentionally. She noted that the edit team had successfully removed another racial slur from the broadcast, but this one slipped through and caused distress. The content was made available on BBC iPlayer after the live airing but was removed around 11:30 on Monday morning following complaints. Attendees had been pre-warned about possible tics from Tourette's, and ho

item.title: UK Requires British Passport or Certificate of Entitlement for Dual Nationals Under New ETA Rules
item.description:
British dual nationals who do not hold a valid British passport may be denied entry to the United Kingdom starting 25 February 2026, as the government begins enforcing its Electronic Travel Authorisation (ETA) scheme. Under the new rules, dual nationals will no longer be able to travel to the UK using only a foreign passport. Instead, they must present either a valid British passport or a Certificate of Entitlement, which costs £589. Without one of these documents, airlines, ferry operators, and train companies may refuse boarding.
Although British and Irish citizens are exempt from applying for an ETA, transport carriers are now required to verify that passengers have appropriate documentation before departure. Since British citizens are not eligible to apply for an ETA or visa, dual nationals must prove their right to enter the UK through official British documentation. Previously, dual nationals could travel on a foreign passport alone, though they sometimes faced delays while Bord

item.title: USS Gerald R. Ford Faces Persistent Sewage System Failures During Extended Deployment Amid U.S.-Iran Tensions
item.description: The USS Gerald R. Ford, the U.S. Navy's most advanced and expensive aircraft carrier at $13.3 billion, is grappling with chronic sewage system problems while deployed in the Middle East. The nuclear-powered vessel, home to over 4,500 sailors, has been at sea since June 2025—now in its eighth month, far exceeding the standard six-month deployment period. This extension, driven by escalating tensions with Iran following President Donald Trump's threats and potential strike preparations, has compounded crew fatigue and mental strain. Many sailors, mostly in their early 20s, report missing significant family events like birthdays, weddings, births, or funerals, leading some to consider leaving the military. The core issue lies in the ship's vacuum-based sewage system, which features narrow pipes prone to calcium buildup and clogs. A single valve failure can disable toilets across entire sections, resulting in most of the 650 toilets being out of service at times. Sailors face daily queues

item.title: UK to Extend Ofcom Broadcasting Rules to Major Streaming Platforms
item.description:
The UK government is introducing new legislation that will require major video-on-demand (VoD) platforms to comply with the same content and accessibility standards as traditional television broadcasters. Under the new rules, streaming services with more than 500,000 UK users — including Netflix, Amazon Prime Video, Disney+, ITVX and Channel 4’s on-demand service — will fall under enhanced regulation by Ofcom.
Previously, only licensed television channels were required to follow Ofcom’s Broadcasting Code, which includes rules on impartial news coverage, harmful or offensive material, and accessibility requirements such as subtitles. Many popular streaming platforms were either subject to lighter oversight or not regulated in the UK at all. The updated framework will designate qualifying services as 'Tier 1' platforms, placing them under a new VoD standards code aligned with existing broadcasting rules.
The changes aim to ensure accurate and impartial news reporting while strengthenin

item.title: Discord ends partnership with Persona after exposed frontend code raises surveillance and data handling concerns
item.description:
Discord has ended a brief partnership with Persona, an identity verification company backed in part by Peter Thiel’s Founders Fund, after researchers discovered that Persona’s frontend code was publicly accessible and hosted on a U.S. government-authorized endpoint. The exposed files, totaling roughly 53 megabytes, revealed that Persona’s systems are capable of conducting extensive verification checks, including facial recognition against watchlists, screening for politically exposed persons, and scanning for adverse media across categories such as terrorism and espionage. Researchers claimed they did not need to exploit vulnerabilities, as the architecture was openly accessible.
Persona CEO Rick Song disputed claims that the exposure constituted a serious security flaw, describing the files as uncompressed frontend code similar to what is already delivered to users’ devices. He emphasized that while the contents may appear alarming, they did not represent a major vulnerability. Song

item.title: Germany's Shift: From Reluctance to Leading Support for Ukraine Amid War
item.description: Germany's response to Russia's invasion of Ukraine has evolved dramatically since 2022. Initially criticized for providing only 5,000 helmets to Kyiv, Berlin is now Ukraine's largest military supporter, supplying advanced weaponry, combat vehicles, and training more than 24,000 Ukrainian soldiers. This transformation reflects both a policy shift and a societal change, as Germany confronts its historical reluctance to engage militarily due to the legacy of World War II and the Cold War. The government implemented a €100 billion fund to strengthen defense capabilities, update contingency plans, and modernize its armed forces. Despite broad public support for military aid, surveys indicate that only a minority of Germans are willing to take up arms themselves. Voluntary military service and increased defense spending have faced protests, particularly among younger generations. Under Chancellor Friedrich Merz, Germany aims to develop the strongest conventional army in Europe, balancing his

item.title: Eddie Jones reflects on England's loss to Ireland and its impact on team morale
item.description: Former England head coach Eddie Jones has compared the atmosphere following England's recent Six Nations defeat against Ireland to the difficult end of his own tenure at Twickenham. England, aiming for their first Six Nations title since 2020, suffered consecutive heavy defeats, including a reversal by Scotland, leaving fans frustrated, some leaving the Allianz Stadium early. Jones described a 'sick feeling' at the game’s end, noting the eerie, half-empty stadium and the crowd’s hostility. He highlighted that assistant coach Steve Borthwick may have erred by publicly setting expectations about a title-deciding match against France, potentially distracting players and influencing team psychology. Jones emphasized that England's dip in performance after a streak of 12 wins stems from issues with emotional control, preparation, leadership, and player buy-in. Additionally, Jones critiqued Borthwick's decision to start Henry Pollock in the back row, suggesting Pollock has more impact in sho

item.title: BBC Radio 4 Special Examines Impact of England’s SEND Reforms on Families and Schools
item.description:
BBC Radio 4’s Woman’s Hour presents a special programme in collaboration with the SEND in the Spotlight podcast, focusing on the UK government’s proposed reforms to Special Educational Needs and Disabilities (SEND) provision in England. The episode explores how the changes could affect parents, young people, teachers, and professionals working within the education system. A key aim of the programme is to clarify complex terminology introduced in the reforms, including terms such as IB, ISP, Targeted, Targeted Plus, and SPP, helping listeners understand what these categories may mean in practice.
Presenter Nuala McGovern speaks with a wide range of contributors, including Schools Standards Minister Georgia Gould MP and the Children’s Commissioner for England, Dame Rachel de Souza. The discussion also features representatives from advocacy groups and charities, such as Marsha Martin of Black SEN Mamas and Katie Ghose, CEO of Kids charity and the Disabled Children’s Partnership. Educatio

item.title: Achraf Hakimi Confirms Upcoming Trial Following Rape Allegation in France
item.description:
Paris St-Germain defender Achraf Hakimi has confirmed that he is set to face trial following an allegation of rape made by a 24-year-old woman. The accusation relates to an incident alleged to have taken place at Hakimi’s home in Paris in 2023. The Morocco international has firmly denied the allegations. The public prosecutor's office in Nanterre, a suburb west of Paris, launched a preliminary investigation in March 2023 after the complaint was filed. Although no trial date has yet been announced, Hakimi publicly addressed the situation on social media, stating that he believes the accusation has led directly to a trial and that he considers the process unjust for innocent individuals while also emphasizing respect for genuine victims. He expressed confidence that the legal proceedings will allow the truth to emerge.
Despite the ongoing legal matter, Hakimi continues to be involved with Paris St-Germain. He was named in the club’s squad for their upcoming Champions League knockout rou

item.title: Russell Brand denies new and previous sexual assault and rape allegations in London court
item.description: Russell Brand, the 50-year-old broadcaster, actor, and comedian, has pleaded not guilty to two additional charges of rape and sexual assault at Southwark Crown Court in London. The alleged incidents are said to have occurred in 2009 and involve two women. Brand had previously pleaded not guilty to two counts of rape, one of indecent assault, and two of sexual assault related to alleged offences between 1999 and 2005, involving four women in London and Bournemouth. The trial for the original charges is scheduled for June, while a hearing to determine if the new allegations will be joined to the same case is set for March. Brand's lawyer requested more time to respond to the new allegations. During the court appearance, Brand wore a leopard print shirt, held a fedora, and carried a Bible with bookmarked pages. The investigations into Brand began following reporting in 2023 by the Sunday Times, The Times, and Channel 4's Dispatches. Brand, originally from Essex, became famous as a stand-u

item.title: Cigarette Sales and Smoking Practices in U.S. Hospitals During the 1950s
item.description:
The article explores a little-known but once common practice in American hospitals during the 1950s: the routine sale and widespread use of cigarettes within medical facilities. At the time, tobacco consumption was deeply embedded in everyday life, and hospitals were no exception. Staff members often wheeled carts through hospital corridors, offering patients cigarettes, snacks, and magazines directly at their bedsides. Patients did not need to leave their rooms to purchase tobacco products, and smoking was permitted in many areas of the hospital, including patient rooms, waiting areas, and even nurses’ stations.
During this era, the health risks associated with smoking were not yet broadly acknowledged by the medical community or the public. In some cases, doctors even permitted or informally recommended smoking, believing it could help calm or comfort patients. Popular unfiltered cigarette brands such as Camels, Lucky Strike, Chesterfield, and Philip Morris were commonly used. Nurse

item.title: UK Student Loan Repayment Threshold to Freeze Despite Modest Average Increase
item.description: Education Secretary Bridget Phillipson has defended the UK government's plan to freeze the repayment threshold for Plan 2 student loans in England, stating that on average graduates will pay just £8 more per month. The threshold will rise to £29,385 in April before being frozen for three years, instead of increasing with inflation. Some graduates, such as Tinuke Bamiro and George Holmes, have expressed concern that student loan repayments combined with higher-rate taxes are affecting their work hours, salaries, and financial planning. Tinuke has increased her pension contributions to reduce taxable income, while George has cut his work hours to save money elsewhere. Plan 2 loans, taken out by undergraduates in England between 2012 and 2023, accrue interest at 6.2% during study and rise later to RPI plus up to 3% depending on earnings. Campaigners and opposition parties are calling for reforms, including lower interest rates, a reversal of threshold freezes, and partial debt forgiveness

item.title: BAFTA Judge Jonte Richardson Resigns Following Controversy Over Racist Slur Incident
item.description: Filmmaker Jonte Richardson has resigned from his role as a BAFTA emerging talent judge, citing the academy's handling of a racial slur incident during the awards ceremony. The slur was uttered involuntarily by John Davidson, a Tourette's campaigner, while black actors Michael B. Jordan and Delroy Lindo were presenting an award. The incident, caused by Davidson's severe Tourette syndrome, was broadcast on live television and streamed on BBC iPlayer before being edited out. Richardson criticized BAFTA for failing to protect the dignity of Black guests and the creative community, calling the organization's response 'utterly unforgivable.' BAFTA issued an apology, taking full responsibility and thanking the actors for their professionalism. The BBC also apologized for not initially censoring the slur. Davidson expressed remorse for the distress his tics caused and left the auditorium early. The film 'I Swear,' which dramatizes Davidson's life with Tourette syndrome, was part of the evening

item.title: Remarkable Trees Across the Globe and Their Unique Features
item.description: Trees are some of the most extraordinary living organisms on Earth, offering not only beauty but also a glimpse into history and survival. This article highlights several remarkable trees from around the world, each with unique characteristics and astonishing longevity. The baobab, often called the 'Tree of Life,' can store up to 120,000 liters of water in its enormous trunk and is found across Africa, Madagascar, and Australia. Mexico's ahuehuete cypress trees have thrived for over 1,400 years, witnessing generations rise and fall beneath their vast canopies. Northern California's coast redwoods are the tallest living trees on Earth, reaching heights over 380 feet and creating awe-inspiring forest environments. Other visually stunning species include dragon blood trees on Socotra Island, Japan's 144-year-old wisteria, and a 1,400-year-old gingko tree in China's Zhongnan Mountains, which showers golden leaves every autumn. These examples illustrate the planet's remarkable tree diversit
